preamble.summary: Enoch sees the flood waters gather for judgment. He learns that these waters serve both to punish the fallen angels and to execute judgment on those they led astray. The angels of punishment prepare to unleash destructive forces. The chapter connects the historical Flood with the eschatological judgment.
preamble.remarkable: The explicit connection between Noah's Flood and the final judgment — both using the same mechanism of cosmic waters — is a distinctive feature of Enochic theology. The Flood was not a one-time event but a prototype of the coming judgment. This typological reading of the Flood appears also in 2 Peter 3:5-7 and Matthew 24:37-39.
preamble.friction: The chapter contains Noah material that some scholars attribute to a separate 'Book of Noah' source embedded within the Parables. The transitions between Enoch's voice and Noah-related material are sometimes abrupt.
preamble.connections: Genesis 7:11 — the Flood mechanism. 2 Peter 3:5-7 — the Flood as prototype of final judgment by fire. Matthew 24:37-39 — 'as in the days of Noah.' 1 Peter 3:20 — Noah and the Flood as types of salvation.

verses.text_reference: And I looked and turned to another part of the earth, and saw there a deep valley with burning fire.
verses.rendering: I looked and turned to another part of the earth and saw a deep valley with burning fire.
translator_notes: Another valley of punishment — the geography of judgment in the Parables includes multiple valleys and abysses. The 'burning fire' is the characteristic element of eschatological punishment throughout Second Temple literature.

verses.text_source: Ge'ez: wa-yāme'u negaśtāt — 'and they brought the kings'
verses.text_reference: And they brought the kings and the mighty, and began to cast them into this deep valley.
verses.rendering: They brought the kings and the mighty and began to cast them into that deep valley.
translator_notes: The kings and mighty — the recurring targets of judgment throughout the Parables — are physically cast into the fiery valley. The verb 'cast' implies forcible removal and powerlessness; these formerly powerful rulers are now helpless.

verses.text_source: Ge'ez: wa-heyya 'a'eyentiya re'iya — 'and there my eyes saw'
verses.text_reference: And there mine eyes saw how they made these their instruments, iron chains of immeasurable weight.
verses.rendering: There my eyes saw them making their instruments — iron chains of immeasurable weight.
translator_notes: The 'immeasurable weight' of the chains emphasizes that no strength can break them. The chains are not ordinary restraints but cosmic fetters designed to hold supernatural beings. This image deeply influenced the chain imagery of Jude 6, 2 Peter 2:4, and Revelation 20:1.

verses.text_source: Ge'ez: wa-se'alku mal'aka — 'and I asked the angel'
verses.text_reference: And I asked the angel of peace who went with me, saying: 'For whom are these chains being prepared?'
verses.rendering: I asked the angel of peace who accompanied me: 'For whom are these chains being prepared?'
translator_notes: The question-and-answer format continues to drive the revelatory narrative.

verses.text_source: Ge'ez: wa-yebēlani — 'and he said to me'
verses.text_reference: And he said unto me: 'These are being prepared for the hosts of Azazel, that they may take them and cast them into the abyss of complete condemnation, and they shall cover their jaws with rough stones as the Lord of Spirits commanded.'
verses.rendering: He told me: 'These are being prepared for the armies of Azazel, to seize them and cast them into the lowest depths of judgment, covering their jaws with rough stones as the Lord of Spirits commanded.'
translator_notes: This largely repeats 53:5. The repetition may be a scribal duplication, or it may serve the literary purpose of framing the intervening material about kings and mighty ones within the Azazel judgment narrative — human and angelic judgment intertwined.

verses.text_source: Ge'ez: wa-Mikā'ēl wa-Gabre'ēl — 'and Michael and Gabriel'
verses.text_reference: And Michael, and Gabriel, and Raphael, and Phanuel shall take hold of them on that great day, and cast them on that day into the burning furnace, that the Lord of Spirits may take vengeance on them for their unrighteousness in becoming subject to Satan and leading astray those who dwell on the earth.
verses.rendering: Michael, Gabriel, Raphael, and Phanuel will seize them on that great day and throw them into the burning furnace, so the Lord of Spirits may exact vengeance on them for their unrighteousness in becoming servants of Satan and leading astray those who dwell on the earth.
translator_notes: Again closely parallels 53:6. The four archangels serve as the executors of divine judgment against the fallen angelic hosts.

verses.text_source: Ge'ez: wa-ba-ye'eti mawā'el — 'and in those days'
verses.text_reference: And in those days shall punishment come from the Lord of Spirits, and he will open all the chambers of waters which are above the heavens, and of the fountains which are beneath the earth.
verses.rendering: In those days punishment will come from the Lord of Spirits. He will open all the water chambers above the heavens and the springs beneath the earth.
translator_notes: The cosmic flood mechanism of Genesis 7:11 is reactivated for eschatological judgment. The Parables treat the Flood not as a completed historical event but as a recurring divine instrument.

verses.text_source: Ge'ez: wa-kwellu māy — 'and all the waters'
verses.text_reference: And all the waters shall be joined with the waters: that which is above the heavens is the masculine, and the water which is beneath the earth is the feminine.
verses.rendering: All the waters will join together: the water above the heavens is the masculine, and the water beneath the earth is the feminine.
translator_notes: The gendering of upper and lower waters is a distinctive cosmological detail. In some ancient Near Eastern cosmologies, the union of upper and lower waters is a creative or destructive act. Here it describes the reunification of the cosmic waters that were separated at creation (Genesis 1:6-7), returning the cosmos to its pre-creation state — uncreation as judgment.

verses.text_reference: And they shall destroy all who dwell on the earth and those who dwell under the ends of the heaven.
verses.rendering: They will destroy all who dwell on the earth and all who live under the ends of heaven.
translator_notes: Universal destruction through cosmic flood — 'all who dwell on the earth' and even those at heaven's edges. The scope is total, echoing Genesis 6:17 ('I am going to bring floodwaters on the earth to destroy all life under the heavens').

verses.text_source: Ge'ez: wa-sobē yā'ammeru — 'and when they recognize'
verses.text_reference: And when they have recognized their unrighteousness which they have wrought on the earth, then by these shall they perish.
verses.rendering: When they recognize the unrighteousness they committed on the earth, then by these waters they will perish.
translator_notes: Recognition of sin comes too late — they acknowledge their wrong only as the judgment falls. This forced recognition parallels Philippians 2:10-11 where every knee bows and every tongue confesses, though in the Parables this recognition does not lead to salvation but accompanies destruction.
